# MASTER CODING STANDARD FOR AI-ASSISTED DEVELOPMENT

**Version:** 4.0 — March 2026 (V3 — Expert Audit)
**Purpose:** Enforce production-grade quality on ALL code generated by LLMs.
**Applies to:** Every file, function, endpoint, and module — no exceptions.
**Languages:** Go 1.22+, TypeScript 5.5+, PHP 8.3+ (language-specific appendices included)
**Sources:** OWASP 2025, OWASP LLM Top 10 2025, COMPASS Benchmark, Veracode 2025, CodeRabbit PR Analysis, SusVibes Benchmark, METR Research, SLSA v1.2, OpenSSF, CISA SBOM 2025, OpenTelemetry
**Companion:** `docs/VIRTUESTACK_KICKSTART_V2.md` — references the SAME quality gates defined here.

> **SINGLE SOURCE OF TRUTH:** This document defines the canonical 19 Quality Gates.
> The Kickstart references them by ID. If there is ever a conflict, THIS document wins.

## 1. PHILOSOPHY & MINDSET

### The Core Problem
AI-generated code has a **"confidence-competence gap"** — it looks complete but collapses under real-world pressure. Statistics from 2025-2026 research:
- AI-authored PRs contain **1.7x more issues** than human-only PRs (CodeRabbit, 470 PRs)
- **62% of AI-generated programs** carry exploitable bugs (FormAI benchmark, 112k programs across 9 models)
- Only **10.5%** of functionally correct AI code is actually secure at repository scale (SusVibes/Carnegie Mellon, Dec 2025)
- **86% XSS failure rate** in AI-generated code (Contrast Security)
- Logic errors are **75% more common**, performance regressions **8x more common**
- **5-22% of AI package suggestions** reference non-existent packages — attackers register these as malware (Slopsquatting, 2025)
- Code quality is **statistically independent** of correctness (r=0.089) and efficiency (r=0.022) — code that works is NOT code that's good (COMPASS Benchmark, Aug 2025)
- **75% of developers** over-trust AI code suggestions despite knowing the risks (Veracode 2025)

### The Solution: Treat All AI Output as Unaudited
Every line of code MUST pass through these gates as if written by an untrusted junior developer. No shortcuts. No "it works on my machine." No TODO placeholders.

### Non-Negotiable Rules
1. **NEVER** leave `TODO`, `FIXME`, or placeholder comments — implement or explicitly document why it's deferred
2. **NEVER** skip error handling on any code path
3. **NEVER** hardcode secrets, API keys, passwords, or environment-specific values
4. **NEVER** use `eval()`, `exec()`, or dynamic code execution with user input
5. **NEVER** trust client-side validation as the sole defense
6. **NEVER** import packages without verifying they exist on official registries and are maintained
7. **NEVER** generate tutorial-style comments explaining basic syntax
8. **NEVER** duplicate code — if logic exists, refactor into shared utilities
9. **NEVER** trust AI-suggested package names without manual verification against the official registry (slopsquatting defense)
10. **ALWAYS** handle null, undefined, empty string, and edge cases explicitly
11. **ALWAYS** implement the complete solution, not a partial proof-of-concept
12. **ALWAYS** verify AI-generated code against OWASP Top 10 before committing

## 2. THE 19 MANDATORY QUALITY GATES

Every piece of code must pass ALL 19 gates before delivery. These gates are the **unified canonical set** — both this document and the VirtueStack Kickstart reference them by the same IDs.

| Gate | Name | Requirement | Failure = Block |
|------|------|------------|----------------|
| QG-01 | **Readable** | Clean architecture, single responsibility, max 3 levels nesting, max 40-line functions | Yes |
| QG-02 | **Secure** | OWASP 2025 Top 10 compliance, zero known vulnerabilities, SSRF prevention, WebSocket security where applicable | Yes |
| QG-03 | **Typed** | Full type safety — no `any` (TS), no `interface{}` without assertion (Go), PHPStan level 8 (PHP) | Yes |
| QG-04 | **Structured** | Proper error handling on every code path — typed errors, no empty catches, no silent failures | Yes |
| QG-05 | **Validated** | Server-side schema validation on every external boundary (request body, query params, headers, webhooks) | Yes |
| QG-06 | **DRY** | No duplicated logic — shared utilities extracted, no copy-paste across files | Yes |
| QG-07 | **Defensive** | Explicit null/undefined/empty/boundary/concurrent state handling — no optimistic assumptions | Yes |
| QG-08 | **Logged** | Structured JSON logging at appropriate levels with correlation IDs, zero PII in logs, trace context propagation | Yes |
| QG-09 | **Bounded** | All external calls have timeouts, circuit breakers, retry with backoff — no unbounded waits | Yes |
| QG-10 | **Clean** | Consistent formatting, linter passes zero warnings, no commented-out code, no console.log/print debug | Yes |
| QG-11 | **Documented** | Public APIs have doc comments, README/INSTALL/USAGE/ARCHITECTURE/RUNBOOK docs included | Yes |
| QG-12 | **Configurable** | All config via environment variables or secret managers — no hardcoded secrets, URLs, or magic numbers | Yes |
| QG-13 | **Compatible** | Backwards compatible changes by default — breaking changes require explicit API versioning with migration guide | Yes |
| QG-14 | **Tested** | Unit + integration tests with meaningful assertions, 80%+ line coverage on business logic | Yes |
| QG-15 | **Dependency-Safe** | Every import verified to exist, actively maintained (updated within 12mo), pinned to exact versions, no hallucinated packages, slopsquatting check performed | Yes |
| QG-16 | **Performant** | No O(n^2+) where O(n) is possible, no N+1 queries, pagination on all list endpoints, no memory leaks | Yes |
| QG-17 | **Provenance-Verified** | Build provenance attestation (SLSA Level 2+), SBOM generated (CycloneDX/SPDX), artifacts signed (Sigstore), lock files committed | Yes |
| QG-18 | **Observable** | Distributed tracing (OpenTelemetry), metrics endpoints, health/readiness probes, trace-log correlation, alerting rules defined | Yes |
| QG-19 | **Deployment-Safe** | Container images hardened (non-root, minimal base, no secrets baked in), graceful shutdown implemented, zero-downtime migration strategy documented | Yes |

### Gate Rationale (Why 19 Instead of 16)

The V2 16-gate model had three critical blind spots:
1. **Supply chain provenance was unverified** — QG-15 checked that packages exist but not that YOUR builds are tamper-proof. QG-17 closes this with SLSA + SBOM + Sigstore.
2. **Observability stopped at logging** — QG-08 covered structured logs but production systems need traces, metrics, and alerting. QG-18 mandates the three pillars.
3. **Deployment security was ignored** — Code could pass all 16 gates and still ship in a root container with secrets baked into the image. QG-19 ensures the deployment artifact is as secure as the code.

## 3. CODE STRUCTURE & READABILITY

### Architecture Rules
- **Single Responsibility Principle**: Each file, class, and function has ONE clear purpose
- **Max function length**: 40 lines (excluding comments/docstrings). If longer, decompose.
- **Max nesting depth**: 3 levels. Use early returns, guard clauses, and extraction.
- **Max file length**: 300 lines. If longer, split into modules.
- **Max parameters**: 4 per function. If more, use an options/config object (Go: use a struct).

### Naming Conventions
- Variables: descriptive, intention-revealing names (`userEmail` not `x`, `ue`, or `data`)
- Functions: verb + noun (`validateUserInput`, `calculateTotalPrice`)
- Booleans: `is`, `has`, `can`, `should` prefix (`isAuthenticated`, `hasPermission`)
- Constants: UPPER_SNAKE_CASE (`MAX_RETRY_COUNT`, `DEFAULT_TIMEOUT_MS`)
- **Acceptable abbreviations:** `id`, `url`, `http`, `ctx`, `err`, `req`, `res`, `db`, `tx`, `ip`, `vm`, `os`, `io`, `rpc`
  - These are universally understood in their respective ecosystems (especially Go)
  - All other abbreviations banned: `usr`, `mgr`, `svc`, `cfg`, `grp` etc. — spell them out

### Code Organization
```
project/
  src/
    config/        # Configuration, env loading, constants
    middleware/     # Auth, validation, rate limiting, logging
    routes/        # Route definitions (thin — delegate to services)
    services/      # Business logic (the core)
    models/        # Data models, schemas, types
    utils/         # Shared pure utility functions
    errors/        # Custom error classes and handlers
  tests/
    unit/           # Unit tests mirroring src/ structure
    integration/    # Integration tests
    e2e/            # End-to-end tests
    fixtures/       # Test data and mocks
  docs/
    INSTALL.md      # Installation guide
    USAGE.md        # Usage guide with examples
    API.md          # API reference
    ARCHITECTURE.md # Architecture decisions
    TESTING.md      # Test and pentest procedures
    RUNBOOK.md      # Incident response runbook (NEW in V3)
  .env.example      # Template for environment variables
  README.md         # Project overview and quickstart
```

### Style Rules
- Use early returns to reduce nesting:
  ```go
  // BAD (Go)
  func Process(user *User) error {
    if user != nil {
      if user.IsActive {
        if user.HasPermission("write") {
          // actual logic buried 3 levels deep
        }
      }
    }
    return nil
  }

  // GOOD (Go)
  func Process(user *User) error {
    if user == nil {
      return ErrNilUser
    }
    if !user.IsActive {
      return ErrInactiveUser
    }
    if !user.HasPermission("write") {
      return ErrForbidden
    }
    // actual logic at top level
    return nil
  }
  ```
- One blank line between logical sections within a function
- Group imports: stdlib > third-party > local (with blank line separators)
- No commented-out code — delete it (that's what version control is for)

---

## 4. SECURITY STANDARDS (OWASP 2025)

Every application MUST address all 10 OWASP 2025 categories:

### A01: Broken Access Control (100% of tested apps affected)
- [ ] Access control enforced server-side ONLY — never trust client
- [ ] Default deny: explicitly grant access, never implicitly allow
- [ ] Centralized authorization middleware reused across all routes
- [ ] Record-level ownership enforced in data models (e.g., `vm.customer_id == jwt.customer_id`)
- [ ] RBAC or ABAC implemented with role hierarchy
- [ ] All user permissions validated server-side per request
- [ ] CORS origins validated strictly (no wildcards in production)
- [ ] JWTs are short-lived (15min access) with proper refresh token rotation
- [ ] Rate limiting on all authenticated endpoints
- [ ] Access control failures logged and alerted
- [ ] **Row-Level Security (RLS)** in PostgreSQL for multi-tenant data isolation
- [ ] **SSRF Prevention** (merged from A10:2021 into A01:2025):
  - Validate and allowlist all URLs before server-side requests
  - Block requests to internal/private IP ranges (10.x, 172.16-31.x, 192.168.x, 169.254.169.254)
  - Block cloud metadata endpoints (`http://169.254.169.254/`, `http://metadata.google.internal/`)
  - Use a dedicated egress proxy for outbound HTTP requests
  - Disable HTTP redirects on server-side requests or re-validate after each redirect
  - DNS rebinding protection: resolve hostname, validate IP, then connect to the resolved IP

### A02: Security Misconfiguration
- [ ] All default credentials changed or disabled
- [ ] Security headers set: CSP, X-Frame-Options, HSTS, X-Content-Type-Options
- [ ] Error responses never expose stack traces, file paths, or system info
- [ ] Debug mode disabled in production configuration
- [ ] Unnecessary HTTP methods disabled
- [ ] Directory listing disabled
- [ ] Sample/test endpoints removed from production builds
- [ ] `Server` and `X-Powered-By` headers removed

### A03: Software Supply Chain Failures (new in 2025 — highest exploit score 8.17)
- [ ] All dependencies pinned to exact versions
- [ ] No packages with known CVEs
- [ ] Transitive dependencies audited
- [ ] Lock files committed and verified
- [ ] SBOM (Software Bill of Materials) generated in CycloneDX 1.5+ or SPDX 3.0+ format
- [ ] Every imported package verified to exist on official registries
- [ ] Minimum maintenance threshold: updated within 12 months, >1000 weekly downloads
- [ ] **Slopsquatting defense:** cross-check every AI-suggested package name against the official registry before adding to dependencies
- [ ] **Build provenance:** SLSA Level 2+ attestation on all build artifacts (see Section 5)
- [ ] **Artifact signing:** all deployable artifacts signed via Sigstore or equivalent
- [ ] **CI/CD pipeline integrity:** build scripts pinned to commit SHAs, no mutable tags in CI workflows

### A04: Cryptographic Failures
- [ ] TLS 1.3 enforced for all data in transit
- [ ] Passwords hashed with **Argon2id** (preferred) or scrypt. **bcrypt is legacy only** — do not use for new code (72-byte input limit silently truncates long passwords)
- [ ] Encryption keys managed via secret manager (never in code)
- [ ] AES-256-GCM for data at rest
- [ ] Cryptographically secure random generators for tokens/keys
- [ ] Certificate validation enabled (no `verify=False`, no `InsecureSkipVerify`)

### A05: Injection (62,445 known CVEs)
- [ ] **Parameterized queries for ALL database operations — no exceptions, no string interpolation**
  ```go
  // CORRECT — parameterized
  db.Exec("UPDATE records SET content = $1 WHERE name = $2", hostname, reverseIP+".in-addr.arpa")

  // WRONG — string interpolation (even in pseudocode, never show this pattern)
  // db.Exec(fmt.Sprintf("UPDATE records SET content='%s' WHERE name='%s'", hostname, reverseIP))
  ```
- [ ] ORM usage with proper escaping
- [ ] Context-aware output encoding for HTML, JS, URL, CSS contexts
- [ ] No `eval()`, `exec()`, or shell command construction with user input
- [ ] Input allowlisting (positive validation) over denylisting
- [ ] Template engines configured for auto-escaping

### A06: Insecure Design
- [ ] Threat model documented for security-critical features
- [ ] Defense in depth: multiple control layers
- [ ] Fail-secure: on error, deny access by default
- [ ] Business logic validated (not just input format)
- [ ] Rate limiting on all API endpoints
- [ ] Trust boundaries documented

### A07: Authentication Failures
- [ ] Use established auth frameworks (never custom auth logic)
- [ ] MFA (TOTP) mandatory for privileged/admin accounts
- [ ] Account lockout after 5-10 failed attempts with progressive delay
- [ ] Session cookies: HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Strict
- [ ] Credentials never logged, cached client-side, or transmitted in URLs
- [ ] Password requirements: minimum 12 characters, check against breached lists
- [ ] **Admin session hardening:** shorter refresh token lifetime (4h, not 7d), re-authentication for destructive operations, concurrent session limits

### A08: Software/Data Integrity Failures
- [ ] Deserialization of untrusted data avoided or sandboxed
- [ ] CI/CD pipeline integrity verified
- [ ] Code signing for deployable artifacts
- [ ] Subresource Integrity (SRI) for CDN-loaded scripts

### A09: Security Logging & Alerting Failures
- [ ] All auth events logged (success and failure)
- [ ] All access control failures logged
- [ ] All input validation failures logged
- [ ] No PII/secrets in log output
- [ ] Structured log format (JSON) with correlation IDs
- [ ] Alert rules for anomalous patterns (repeated failures, privilege escalation)

### A10: Mishandling of Exceptional Conditions (new in 2025)
- [ ] Every code path has explicit error handling
- [ ] Timeouts set on all external calls (HTTP, DB, message queues, gRPC)
- [ ] Circuit breakers for external service dependencies (default: open after **5 consecutive failures**; project-specific overrides documented in kickstart)
- [ ] Resource cleanup in `finally` blocks or Go `defer` statements
- [ ] Null/undefined checks before every property access on external data
- [ ] Graceful degradation under partial system failures

---

## 5. DEPENDENCY & SUPPLY CHAIN SECURITY

> **Significantly expanded in V3.** V2 covered only "verify packages exist." V3 adds SLSA provenance, SBOM generation, Sigstore signing, slopsquatting defense, and CI/CD pipeline integrity — reflecting OWASP 2025 A03's highest exploit score (8.17) and real-world incidents (SolarWinds, XZ Utils, Shai-Hulud npm worm).

### Dependency Rules
1. **Justify every dependency** — If stdlib or <50 lines of code can replace it, don't add the package
2. **Verify existence** — Check the package actually exists on the official registry before importing
3. **Check maintenance status** — Last update within 12 months, >1000 weekly downloads, no known CVEs
4. **Pin exact versions** — Use `==` (Python), exact versions in `go.mod`, no `^` or `~` in production (Node)
5. **Audit transitive deps** — Run `npm audit` / `pip-audit` / `govulncheck` and resolve all findings
6. **Commit lock files** — `package-lock.json`, `go.sum`, `composer.lock` are mandatory
7. **Separate dev dependencies** — Test/build tools must not ship to production

### Slopsquatting Defense (NEW in V3)
Slopsquatting is when AI hallucinates a package name that doesn't exist, and an attacker registers it on npm/PyPI/Go with malware. **5-22% of AI-generated package suggestions are vulnerable.**

**Mandatory checks before adding ANY AI-suggested dependency:**
1. Search the exact package name on the official registry (npmjs.com, pkg.go.dev, pypi.org)
2. Verify the package has >100 stars, >1000 weekly downloads, and multiple contributors
3. Check the package creation date — packages created very recently after AI model training cutoffs are suspicious
4. Compare the suggested name against known legitimate packages (typosquatting check)
5. Use SCA (Software Composition Analysis) tools in CI to automatically flag unknown packages

```bash
# Automated slopsquatting check in CI
npm pack --dry-run 2>&1 | grep -E "^npm warn" # Check for unknown packages
pip install --dry-run <package> 2>&1            # Verify package resolves
```

### Build Provenance (SLSA v1.2) (NEW in V3)

All production builds MUST achieve **SLSA Level 2** minimum:

| SLSA Level | Requirement | Status |
|------------|------------|--------|
| Level 1 | Build process documented, SBOM generated | Minimum for dev/staging |
| Level 2 | Signed provenance from a hosted build service, tamper-resistant | **Required for production** |
| Level 3 | Isolated build environment, build-level security enforcement | Recommended for critical services |

**Implementation:**
```yaml
# GitHub Actions — SLSA Build Provenance
- uses: actions/attest-build-provenance@v2
  with:
    subject-path: 'dist/my-binary'
    # Uses Sigstore keyless signing (ephemeral keys, OIDC identity)
```

### SBOM Generation (NEW in V3)
Every release MUST include a machine-readable SBOM:
- **Format:** CycloneDX 1.5+ (security-focused) or SPDX 3.0+ (license-focused)
- **Contents:** All direct and transitive dependencies with exact versions, hashes (SHA-256 minimum), and package URLs (PURL)
- **Delivery:** Attached as a build artifact alongside the release binary
- **Update cadence:** Regenerate SBOM on every dependency change, not just major releases

```bash
# Go
cyclonedx-gomod app -json -output sbom.json

# Node.js
npx @cyclonedx/cyclonedx-npm --output-file sbom.json

# PHP
composer require --dev cyclonedx/cyclonedx-php-composer
composer make-bom --output-file=sbom.json
```

### Artifact Signing (Sigstore) (NEW in V3)
- All container images signed with `cosign` (Sigstore keyless signing)
- All binaries signed with provenance attestation
- Verification step in deployment pipeline rejects unsigned artifacts

```bash
# Sign container image (keyless via Sigstore)
cosign sign --yes ghcr.io/myorg/myapp:v1.2.3

# Verify before deployment
cosign verify ghcr.io/myorg/myapp:v1.2.3 \
  --certificate-identity-regexp='.*' \
  --certificate-oidc-issuer='https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com'
```

### CI/CD Pipeline Integrity
- **Pin all CI actions to commit SHAs**, not mutable tags (`uses: actions/checkout@abc123` not `@v4`)
- **Require code review** on CI/CD configuration changes (`.github/workflows/`, `Jenkinsfile`, etc.)
- **Restrict secrets** to specific workflow steps — never expose to arbitrary scripts
- **Audit CI logs** for unexpected network calls or package installations
- **CODEOWNERS enforcement** on build pipeline files

### Banned Patterns
- `import *` — Always import specific names
- Importing entire utility libraries for one function (e.g., all of lodash for `_.get`)
- Using deprecated packages with maintained alternatives
- Packages with fewer than 100 GitHub stars and single maintainer (high supply chain risk)
- **Hallucinated imports** — packages that don't exist. When uncertain, use stdlib alternatives.
- **Mutable tags in CI** — `uses: actions/checkout@v4` (use SHA instead)

---

## 6. ERROR HANDLING & RESILIENCE

### Architecture
```
Application Layer:
  +-- Custom Error Types (typed, categorized, with error codes)
  +-- Error Handler Middleware (centralized)
  +-- Structured Error Responses (consistent format)
  +-- Recovery Strategies (retry, fallback, circuit break)
  +-- Operation Journal (for multi-step operations)
```

### Requirements
1. **Custom error types** for each error category:
   - `ValidationError` — Invalid input (400)
   - `AuthenticationError` — Auth failed (401)
   - `ForbiddenError` — Insufficient permissions (403)
   - `NotFoundError` — Resource not found (404)
   - `ConflictError` — State conflict (409)
   - `RateLimitError` — Too many requests (429)
   - `InternalError` — Unexpected server error (500)

   **Go-specific:** Use sentinel errors + custom error types implementing the `error` interface:
   ```go
   var ErrNotFound = errors.New("resource not found")

   type ValidationError struct {
     Field   string
     Message string
   }
   func (e *ValidationError) Error() string {
     return fmt.Sprintf("validation: %s — %s", e.Field, e.Message)
   }
   ```

2. **Centralized error handler** that:
   - Catches all unhandled exceptions/panics
   - Logs full error details server-side (stack trace, context)
   - Returns sanitized error response to client (no internals exposed)
   - Includes correlation ID for debugging
   - Maps to correct HTTP status code

3. **Consistent error response format**:
   ```json
   {
     "error": {
       "code": "VALIDATION_ERROR",
       "message": "Email format is invalid",
       "details": [{"field": "email", "issue": "Must be a valid email address"}],
       "correlationId": "req_abc123"
     }
   }
   ```

4. **Resilience patterns**:
   - Timeouts on ALL external calls (default: 10s HTTP, 5s DB, 30s batch, 5s gRPC unary, 60s gRPC stream)
   - Retry with exponential backoff for transient failures (max 3 retries)
   - **Circuit breaker** for external services:
     - **Default threshold: 5 consecutive failures** (global standard)
     - Project-specific overrides are allowed and must be documented (e.g., VirtueStack node heartbeat uses 3 failures matching the NODE_FAILED threshold)
     - Half-open state: attempt one request after cooldown period (default 30s)
   - Graceful degradation: if a non-critical service fails, degrade feature, don't crash
   - Resource cleanup: database connections, file handles, temp files always released

5. **Operation journal for multi-step operations**:
   - Every multi-step mutation (VM create, migration, failover) writes its state to the database before each step
   - On recovery, the system replays or rolls back incomplete operations
   - States: `pending -> step_1_complete -> step_2_complete -> ... -> completed | failed | rolled_back`

6. **Banned error patterns**:
   - Empty catch blocks (`catch (e) {}` or bare `recover()` — silent failure)
   - Catching generic exceptions without re-throwing or logging
   - Returning `null` to indicate error (use Result type or return error)
   - Using error strings instead of error types for control flow
   - Go: ignoring returned errors with `_` without explicit comment justifying why

---

## 7. INPUT VALIDATION & OUTPUT ENCODING

### Input Validation (Server-Side — Mandatory)

ALL external data MUST be validated before processing. External data includes:
- Request body, query parameters, URL parameters, headers, cookies
- File uploads (name, size, type, content)
- Webhook payloads
- Data from third-party APIs
- Database results (defensive coding)
- **WebSocket messages** (size, format, rate)

**Validation Strategy:**
1. **Schema validation** using a library (Zod/TypeScript, go-playground/validator/Go, Pydantic/Python)
2. **Type checking** — Verify types before operations
3. **Range checking** — Min/max for numbers, length limits for strings
4. **Format validation** — Regex for emails, UUIDs, dates (use established patterns)
5. **Allowlisting** — Accept known-good values, reject everything else
6. **Sanitization** — Strip dangerous characters AFTER validation

**Example (Go with validator):**
```go
type CreateVMRequest struct {
  PlanID     uuid.UUID `json:"plan_id" validate:"required"`
  TemplateID uuid.UUID `json:"template_id" validate:"required"`
  Hostname   string    `json:"hostname" validate:"required,min=1,max=63,hostname_rfc1123"`
  SSHKey     string    `json:"ssh_key" validate:"omitempty,max=4096"`
}

func (h *Handler) CreateVM(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
  var req CreateVMRequest
  if err := json.NewDecoder(r.Body).Decode(&req); err != nil {
    respondError(w, NewValidationError("invalid JSON body"))
    return
  }
  if err := h.validator.Struct(req); err != nil {
    respondError(w, NewValidationError(err.Error()))
    return
  }
  // Only use validated req from here
}
```

**Example (TypeScript with Zod):**
```typescript
const CreateUserSchema = z.object({
  email: z.string().email().max(254),
  name: z.string().min(1).max(100).regex(/^[a-zA-Z\s'-]+$/),
  age: z.number().int().min(13).max(150),
  role: z.enum(['user', 'admin', 'moderator']),
});

const result = CreateUserSchema.safeParse(req.body);
if (!result.success) {
  throw new ValidationError(result.error.issues);
}
```

### Output Encoding
- HTML context: HTML entity encoding
- JavaScript context: JavaScript Unicode encoding
- URL context: Percent encoding
- CSS context: CSS hex encoding
- SQL context: Parameterized queries (not encoding)
- Use template engines with auto-escaping enabled

---

## 8. AUTHENTICATION & ACCESS CONTROL

### Authentication
- Use established libraries (never custom crypto, token generation, or password hashing logic)
- JWT tokens: short-lived access (15min), longer refresh (7d for customers, **4h for admins**), rotation on use
- Store refresh tokens server-side (database), not in localStorage
- Session cookies: `HttpOnly`, `Secure`, `SameSite=Strict`
- Implement CSRF protection: **Double-submit cookie pattern** for APIs using cookie-based auth. If `SameSite=Strict` is enforced AND all APIs are same-origin, document that SameSite alone suffices with explicit rationale.
- Password storage: **Argon2id only for new projects.** bcrypt is legacy — allowed only in existing codebases with a migration plan to Argon2id.

### Admin-Specific Session Hardening
- **Shorter refresh token lifetime:** 4 hours (not 7 days)
- **Re-authentication required** for destructive operations: HA failover, node drain, customer deletion, system config changes
- **Concurrent session limit:** max 3 active admin sessions per account
- **Session binding:** log IP and user-agent per session; alert on mid-session changes
- **Admin audit log:** all session events (login, logout, token refresh, re-auth) logged with IP, user-agent, geolocation

### Authorization
- Middleware-based: check permissions BEFORE reaching business logic
- Resource-level: verify ownership (`user.id === resource.ownerId`)
- Field-level: sensitive fields only returned for authorized roles
- Consistent: same authorization logic for API, WebSocket, and background jobs
- **Multi-tenant isolation:** PostgreSQL Row-Level Security (RLS) policies enforce customer data boundaries at the database level, not just application level
- **Consider externalized authorization** (OPA, OpenFGA, Cedar) for complex RBAC/ABAC requirements — separates policy from code

### API Versioning Strategy (NEW in V3)
When breaking changes are unavoidable, use explicit API versioning:

**Preferred: URL Path Versioning**
```
/api/v1/vms        # Original
/api/v2/vms        # Breaking change — new response shape
```

**Acceptable: Header-Based Versioning**
```
Accept: application/vnd.myapp.v2+json
API-Version: 2
```

**Versioning Rules:**
1. **Support N-1 versions minimum** — when v3 ships, v2 must still work
2. **Deprecation timeline:** announce deprecation 6 months before removal; include `Sunset` and `Deprecation` headers
3. **Migration guide required** for every breaking change: document what changed, provide before/after examples, automate migration where possible
4. **Version in response:** include `X-API-Version` header in all responses so clients know which version served them
5. **Never version for additive changes** — new fields, new endpoints, and new optional parameters are NOT breaking changes

### Rate Limiting
- Global: 100 req/min per IP for public endpoints
- Auth: 5 attempts per 15min for login endpoints
- API: Tiered by user plan with headers (X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining)
- Implement sliding window algorithm (not fixed window)
- **Infrastructure-level:** reverse proxy (nginx/Caddy) connection limits, SYN flood protection
- **Expensive operations:** stricter limits on resource-intensive endpoints (VM create, live migration, backup trigger)

---

## 9. CRYPTOGRAPHY & SECRETS

### Secret Management
- ALL secrets via environment variables or secret manager (Vault, AWS SSM, etc.)
- `.env.example` with placeholder values committed (never `.env` itself)
- `.env` in `.gitignore` always
- Different secrets per environment (dev, staging, production)
- Key rotation plan documented

### Automatic Secrets Rotation (NEW in V3)
V2 said "key rotation plan documented" — V3 mandates concrete rotation patterns:

| Secret Type | Rotation Frequency | Method |
|-------------|-------------------|--------|
| Database credentials | 90 days | Vault dynamic secrets or AWS RDS IAM auth |
| API keys (internal) | 90 days | Dual-key overlap: generate new key, update consumers, revoke old key |
| JWT signing keys | 30 days | JWKS rotation: add new key to set, sign with new, keep old for verification until tokens expire |
| TLS certificates | Auto-renew 30 days before expiry | cert-manager (K8s) or Let's Encrypt ACME |
| Encryption keys (data at rest) | Annually | Envelope encryption: rotate KEK, re-wrap DEKs without re-encrypting data |

**Implementation Pattern:**
```go
// JWKS rotation — always verify against key set, not single key
func (v *JWTVerifier) Verify(token string) (*Claims, error) {
  keySet, err := v.jwksClient.GetKeys() // Returns all active keys
  if err != nil {
    return nil, fmt.Errorf("fetching JWKS: %w", err)
  }
  // Try each key — allows overlap during rotation
  for _, key := range keySet.Keys {
    claims, err := jwt.Verify(token, key)
    if err == nil {
      return claims, nil
    }
  }
  return nil, ErrInvalidToken
}
```

### Zero-Trust Service-to-Service Authentication (NEW in V3)
- **mTLS for all internal service communication** — no plaintext HTTP between services, even within a private network
- **Service identity:** each service has its own identity (SPIFFE ID or K8s ServiceAccount) — no shared credentials
- **Short-lived credentials:** service tokens expire in minutes, not hours; use workload identity federation where possible
- **Network segmentation is NOT sufficient** — authenticate and authorize every request regardless of network position

### Cryptographic Standards
| Use Case | Algorithm | Status | Never Use |
|----------|-----------|--------|-----------|
| Password hashing | **Argon2id** | Preferred for all new code | MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256 (plain), DES |
| Password hashing | bcrypt | **Legacy only** — 72-byte input limit silently truncates long passwords. Existing codebases must have migration plan to Argon2id. | — |
| Password hashing | scrypt | Acceptable alternative | — |
| Symmetric encryption | AES-256-GCM | Required | AES-ECB, DES, RC4, Blowfish |
| Hashing (non-password) | SHA-256, SHA-3 | Required | MD5, SHA-1 |
| Token generation | `crypto/rand` (Go), `crypto.randomBytes(32)` (Node) | Required | `math/rand`, `Math.random()`, timestamps |
| TLS | TLS 1.3 (minimum 1.2) | Required | SSL, TLS 1.0, TLS 1.1 |
| Key exchange | ECDH (P-256+), X25519 | Required | RSA-1024, DH-1024 |

---

## 10. LOGGING, TRACING & OBSERVABILITY

> **Rewritten in V3.** V2 covered only structured logging. V3 mandates the three pillars of observability: logs, traces, and metrics — all correlated via OpenTelemetry.

### The Three Pillars

| Pillar | Purpose | Tool | Standard |
|--------|---------|------|----------|
| **Logs** | Discrete events, audit trail, debugging | Structured JSON (zerolog/Go, pino/Node, monolog/PHP) | Correlation ID + Trace ID in every log line |
| **Traces** | Request flow across services, latency breakdown | OpenTelemetry SDK | W3C Trace Context propagation |
| **Metrics** | Aggregated measurements, alerting thresholds | OpenTelemetry Metrics / Prometheus | RED metrics (Rate, Errors, Duration) for every endpoint |

### Structured Logging Requirements

**Every log entry MUST include:**
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-12T08:15:30.123Z",
  "level": "error",
  "message": "payment processing failed",
  "service": "billing-service",
  "correlationId": "req_abc123",
  "traceId": "4bf92f3577b34da6a3ce929d0e0e4736",
  "spanId": "00f067aa0ba902b7",
  "userId": "usr_masked_hash",
  "error": {
    "type": "PaymentGatewayTimeout",
    "message": "upstream timeout after 10s"
  },
  "duration_ms": 10042
}
```

**Log Levels:**
| Level | Use For |
|-------|---------|
| `error` | Failures requiring immediate attention — failed payments, data corruption, unhandled errors |
| `warn` | Degraded behavior — circuit breaker opened, retry attempted, approaching rate limits |
| `info` | Business events — user created, order placed, deployment completed |
| `debug` | Development-only details — query parameters, intermediate state (NEVER in production) |

**Log Hygiene:**
- NEVER log: passwords, tokens, API keys, credit card numbers, SSNs, full email addresses, session IDs
- ALWAYS log: correlation ID, trace ID, user ID (hashed), operation name, duration, result (success/failure)
- Mask PII: `user@example.com` -> `u***@e***.com`
- Rate-limit repetitive error logs (max 1 per second for same error class)
- Include enough context to reproduce the issue without accessing the database

### Distributed Tracing (OpenTelemetry) (NEW in V3)

All services MUST instrument traces using OpenTelemetry SDK:

**Go Example:**
```go
import (
  "go.opentelemetry.io/otel"
  "go.opentelemetry.io/otel/attribute"
  "go.opentelemetry.io/otel/trace"
)

var tracer = otel.Tracer("billing-service")

func (s *BillingService) ProcessPayment(ctx context.Context, req *PaymentRequest) (*PaymentResult, error) {
  ctx, span := tracer.Start(ctx, "ProcessPayment",
    trace.WithAttributes(
      attribute.String("payment.method", req.Method),
      attribute.Float64("payment.amount", req.Amount),
      attribute.String("payment.currency", req.Currency),
    ),
  )
  defer span.End()

  // Downstream calls automatically propagate trace context
  result, err := s.gateway.Charge(ctx, req)
  if err != nil {
    span.RecordError(err)
    span.SetStatus(codes.Error, err.Error())
    return nil, fmt.Errorf("charging payment: %w", err)
  }

  span.SetAttributes(attribute.String("payment.transaction_id", result.TransactionID))
  return result, nil
}
```

**What to trace:**
- Every incoming HTTP/gRPC request (auto-instrumented via middleware)
- Every outgoing HTTP/gRPC call
- Every database query (with sanitized query text, not parameters)
- Every message queue publish/consume
- Every cache hit/miss
- Custom business operations (payment processing, VM provisioning, etc.)

**Trace-Log Correlation:**
- Extract `traceId` and `spanId` from the active span and inject into every log entry
- This allows jumping from a log line directly to the full distributed trace

### Metrics (NEW in V3)

Every service MUST expose:

**RED Metrics (per endpoint):**
- **Rate:** requests per second
- **Errors:** error rate (4xx, 5xx)
- **Duration:** latency histogram (p50, p95, p99)

**USE Metrics (per resource):**
- **Utilization:** CPU, memory, disk, connection pool usage
- **Saturation:** queue depth, thread pool exhaustion, pending requests
- **Errors:** resource-level errors (connection failures, OOM kills)

**Implementation:**
```go
// Prometheus-style metrics via OpenTelemetry
meter := otel.Meter("billing-service")

requestDuration, _ := meter.Float64Histogram(
  "http.server.request.duration",
  metric.WithUnit("s"),
  metric.WithDescription("HTTP request duration in seconds"),
)

requestCount, _ := meter.Int64Counter(
  "http.server.request.total",
  metric.WithDescription("Total HTTP requests"),
)
```

### Health & Readiness Probes (NEW in V3)

Every service MUST expose:
- `GET /healthz` — **Liveness probe:** is the process alive? (returns 200 if process is running)
- `GET /readyz` — **Readiness probe:** can it accept traffic? (checks DB connection, cache, downstream dependencies)
- `GET /metrics` — Prometheus-compatible metrics endpoint

```go
func (h *HealthHandler) Readyz(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
  checks := map[string]error{
    "database": h.db.PingContext(r.Context()),
    "cache":    h.cache.Ping(r.Context()),
    "queue":    h.queue.Ping(r.Context()),
  }
  for name, err := range checks {
    if err != nil {
      w.WriteHeader(http.StatusServiceUnavailable)
      json.NewEncoder(w).Encode(map[string]string{"status": "unhealthy", "failing": name})
      return
    }
  }
  json.NewEncoder(w).Encode(map[string]string{"status": "healthy"})
}
```

### Alerting Rules (NEW in V3)

Every production service MUST define alerts for:
- Error rate > 5% over 5 minutes
- p99 latency > 2x baseline over 5 minutes
- Health check failures for > 30 seconds
- Circuit breaker open events
- Disk usage > 80%
- Certificate expiry < 14 days

Alert definitions MUST be committed as code (Prometheus rules, Grafana alerts, or equivalent).

---

## 11. PERFORMANCE & SCALABILITY

### Database Performance
- **Indexing:** Every `WHERE`, `JOIN`, and `ORDER BY` column must have an appropriate index
- **No N+1 queries:** Use `JOIN`, `IN`, or batch loading — never query in a loop
- **Pagination:** ALL list endpoints use cursor-based pagination (preferred) or offset/limit
- **Connection pooling:** Mandatory for all database connections (Go: `sql.DB` with `SetMaxOpenConns`, `SetMaxIdleConns`, `SetConnMaxLifetime`)
- **Query timeouts:** Statement-level timeouts via `context.WithTimeout` (5s default for OLTP, 30s for reports)
- **EXPLAIN ANALYZE:** Run on all new queries against production-scale data before merging

### Zero-Downtime Database Migrations (NEW in V3)

All schema changes MUST follow the **expand-contract pattern** for zero-downtime deployments:

**Phase 1: Expand** (backwards compatible)
- Add new columns as nullable or with defaults
- Create new tables
- Add new indexes concurrently (`CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY` in PostgreSQL)
- NEVER rename or drop columns/tables in this phase

**Phase 2: Migrate**
- Deploy code that writes to both old and new schema
- Backfill data from old columns to new
- Verify data integrity

**Phase 3: Contract** (after all readers updated)
- Remove old column reads from code
- Drop old columns/tables in a subsequent migration

```sql
-- GOOD: Expand phase — add column with default
ALTER TABLE vms ADD COLUMN region_id UUID;
ALTER TABLE vms ALTER COLUMN region_id SET DEFAULT gen_random_uuid();

-- GOOD: Concurrent index (non-blocking)
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_vms_region ON vms(region_id);

-- BAD: Blocking rename (causes downtime)
-- ALTER TABLE vms RENAME COLUMN datacenter TO region_id;
```

**Migration safety rules:**
- Every migration MUST have a rollback script
- Migrations MUST be idempotent (safe to run twice)
- Lock timeout: `SET lock_timeout = '5s'` to avoid blocking the table for long periods
- Test migrations against a production-size dataset before deploying

### Feature Flags & Progressive Rollout (NEW in V3)

For risky changes, use feature flags to control rollout:

```go
// Feature flag check
if featureflags.IsEnabled(ctx, "new-billing-engine", user.ID) {
  return s.newBillingEngine.Process(ctx, req)
}
return s.legacyBillingEngine.Process(ctx, req)
```

**Rules:**
- Feature flags MUST have an owner and expiry date (no permanent flags — that's a code branch)
- Clean up flags within 30 days of 100% rollout
- Flags MUST be server-evaluated (never send flag logic to client)
- Rollout stages: 1% -> 10% -> 50% -> 100% with monitoring between each stage
- Rollback capability: any flag can be disabled instantly without deployment

### API Performance
- **Response time targets:** p50 < 100ms, p95 < 500ms, p99 < 1000ms for CRUD operations
- **Compression:** gzip/brotli for responses > 1KB
- **Caching:** HTTP cache headers (`ETag`, `Cache-Control`) for read-heavy endpoints
- **Field selection:** Support `?fields=id,name,status` to reduce payload size
- **Batch endpoints:** Provide batch APIs for operations commonly done in loops

### Algorithmic Complexity
- No O(n^2) or worse where O(n) or O(n log n) alternatives exist
- No unbounded memory allocation (always cap collections, use streaming for large datasets)
- Profile before optimizing — measure, don't guess
- Document complexity for non-obvious algorithms

### Memory Management
- Go: use `sync.Pool` for frequently allocated objects, watch for goroutine leaks
- Node.js: avoid closure-based memory leaks, use `WeakRef`/`WeakMap` for caches
- Set memory limits in container specs; handle OOM gracefully
- Stream large files — never load entire file into memory

---

## 12. CONCURRENCY, PARALLELISM & LIFECYCLE

### Go Concurrency Rules
- **Every goroutine must have a cancellation path** via `context.Context`
- **Never fire-and-forget goroutines** — always track with `sync.WaitGroup` or `errgroup.Group`
- **Channel direction** specified in function signatures (`chan<-` for send-only, `<-chan` for receive-only)
- **Select with context.Done()** in every long-running goroutine
- **Mutex scope** minimized — lock only what needs protection, unlock ASAP (prefer `defer mu.Unlock()` for safety)
- **No goroutine leaks:** use `goleak` in tests to verify

```go
// GOOD: errgroup with context cancellation
func (s *Service) ProcessBatch(ctx context.Context, items []Item) error {
  g, ctx := errgroup.WithContext(ctx)
  g.SetLimit(10) // Bound concurrency

  for _, item := range items {
    item := item // capture loop variable (Go < 1.22)
    g.Go(func() error {
      return s.processItem(ctx, item)
    })
  }
  return g.Wait()
}
```

### TypeScript Concurrency Rules
- Use `Promise.allSettled()` over `Promise.all()` when partial failure is acceptable
- Always handle promise rejections — no unhandled promise rejection warnings
- Use `AbortController` for cancellable async operations
- Worker threads for CPU-intensive work — never block the event loop
- Connection pool limits for database and HTTP clients

### Graceful Shutdown (NEW in V3)

All services MUST handle shutdown signals properly for containerized deployments:

```go
func main() {
  srv := &http.Server{Addr: ":8080", Handler: router}

  // Start server
  go func() {
    if err := srv.ListenAndServe(); err != nil && err != http.ErrServerClosed {
      log.Fatal().Err(err).Msg("server failed")
    }
  }()

  // Wait for SIGTERM/SIGINT
  quit := make(chan os.Signal, 1)
  signal.Notify(quit, syscall.SIGTERM, syscall.SIGINT)
  <-quit

  log.Info().Msg("shutting down gracefully...")

  // Grace period for in-flight requests
  ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), 30*time.Second)
  defer cancel()

  // Stop accepting new requests, wait for in-flight to complete
  if err := srv.Shutdown(ctx); err != nil {
    log.Error().Err(err).Msg("forced shutdown")
  }

  // Close other resources
  db.Close()
  cache.Close()
  tracer.Shutdown(ctx)

  log.Info().Msg("shutdown complete")
}
```

**Shutdown requirements:**
1. **Stop accepting new requests** immediately on SIGTERM
2. **Complete in-flight requests** within a grace period (default: 30s, must match K8s `terminationGracePeriodSeconds`)
3. **Drain connections:** close idle connections, wait for active ones
4. **Flush buffers:** ensure log buffers, trace exporters, and metric collectors flush before exit
5. **Close resources:** database connections, message queue consumers, file handles
6. **Exit cleanly** with code 0 on success, non-zero on forced shutdown

### Race Condition Prevention
- Use `go test -race` for every CI run
- Database: optimistic locking (`version` column) for concurrent updates
- Distributed: advisory locks or Redis-based distributed locks with TTL
- Idempotency keys for all mutation APIs (client-generated UUID per request)

---

## 13. TESTING REQUIREMENTS

### Test Coverage
- **Minimum 80% line coverage** on business logic (services, handlers, middleware)
- **100% coverage** on security-critical paths (auth, authorization, input validation, crypto)
- Unit tests: isolated, fast, no external dependencies (mock all I/O)
- Integration tests: real database (test container), real Redis, real message queue
- E2E tests: critical user flows (signup, payment, core CRUD)

### Test Structure
```go
// Go table-driven tests (mandatory pattern)
func TestValidateHostname(t *testing.T) {
  tests := []struct {
    name    string
    input   string
    wantErr bool
  }{
    {"valid hostname", "web-server-01", false},
    {"empty string", "", true},
    {"too long", strings.Repeat("a", 64), true},
    {"special chars", "server@01", true},
    {"starts with hyphen", "-server", true},
    {"unicode", "serveur-francais", false},
  }
  for _, tt := range tests {
    t.Run(tt.name, func(t *testing.T) {
      err := ValidateHostname(tt.input)
      if (err != nil) != tt.wantErr {
        t.Errorf("ValidateHostname(%q) error = %v, wantErr %v", tt.input, err, tt.wantErr)
      }
    })
  }
}
```

### Mandatory Test Cases
- Happy path (valid input -> expected output)
- Invalid input (missing fields, wrong types, boundary values)
- Edge cases (empty collections, max values, Unicode, concurrent access)
- Auth/authz (unauthenticated, wrong role, expired token, revoked session)
- Error conditions (DB down, timeout, partial failure)
- Idempotency (same request twice produces same result)
- Race conditions (use `-race` flag in Go CI)

### Test Anti-Patterns (Banned)
- Tests that test implementation details instead of behavior
- Tests with no assertions ("smoke tests" that just don't crash)
- Tests that depend on execution order
- Tests that depend on real time (`time.Sleep`) — use fake clocks
- Tests that share mutable state between test cases
- Snapshot tests for dynamic data (timestamps, UUIDs)

---

## 14. DOCUMENTATION REQUIREMENTS

### Required Documents
Every project MUST include:

| Document | Contents | Update Frequency |
|----------|----------|------------------|
| `README.md` | Project overview, architecture diagram (Mermaid), tech stack, quickstart (clone-to-running in <5 commands) | Every feature change |
| `docs/INSTALL.md` | Prerequisites, step-by-step setup for dev/staging/prod, troubleshooting FAQ | Every dependency change |
| `docs/USAGE.md` | User-facing guide with examples, common workflows, configuration reference | Every feature change |
| `docs/API.md` | Complete API reference (auto-generated from OpenAPI spec where possible) | Every API change |
| `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` | System design, component diagram, data flow, technology choices with rationale | Major changes |
| `docs/TESTING.md` | How to run tests, how to write tests, coverage expectations, pentest procedures | Setup or CI changes |
| `docs/RUNBOOK.md` | Incident response procedures (NEW in V3 — see below) | Every operational change |
| `.env.example` | All environment variables with descriptions and example values | Every config change |

### API Documentation
- Every public function, method, and type has a doc comment
- Go: GoDoc-compatible comments (starts with function name)
- TypeScript: JSDoc with `@param`, `@returns`, `@throws`, `@example`
- REST APIs: OpenAPI 3.1 spec maintained alongside code
- WebSocket APIs: message schemas documented with examples for each event type

### Architecture Decision Records (ADRs)
For significant technical decisions, create an ADR:
```markdown
# ADR-001: Use Argon2id for Password Hashing

## Status: Accepted
## Date: 2026-03-01

## Context
bcrypt has a 72-byte input limit that silently truncates long passwords.

## Decision
Use Argon2id for all new password hashing. Existing bcrypt hashes will be
migrated on next login.

## Consequences
- Positive: No silent truncation, memory-hard (resistant to GPU attacks)
- Negative: Slightly higher memory usage per hash operation
```

### Incident Response Runbook (NEW in V3)

`docs/RUNBOOK.md` MUST include:

1. **Escalation paths:** who to contact for what (on-call rotation, service owners, management)
2. **Common failure scenarios:**
   - Database connection exhaustion — diagnosis steps, mitigation
   - High error rate spike — investigation checklist
   - Memory leak detection — how to capture heap profile, analyze
   - Certificate expiry — emergency renewal procedure
   - Dependency outage — circuit breaker behavior, manual overrides
3. **Recovery procedures:**
   - How to roll back a deployment
   - How to restore from backup
   - How to manually drain a node
   - How to force-reset a circuit breaker
4. **Post-incident template:**
   - Timeline, root cause, impact, action items, prevention measures

---

## 15. POST-CODING: BUG TESTING CHECKLIST

Run this checklist against EVERY deliverable before marking complete:

### Functional Tests
- [ ] All CRUD operations work with valid data
- [ ] Invalid input returns appropriate error messages (not 500)
- [ ] Edge cases handled: empty strings, zero values, max-length inputs, null fields
- [ ] Pagination works correctly (first page, last page, empty results, out-of-range)
- [ ] Sorting and filtering return correct results
- [ ] Concurrent operations don't corrupt data (test with `-race` flag)
- [ ] Idempotent operations return same result on retry

### State Management
- [ ] Application recovers correctly after restart
- [ ] Partial failures don't leave orphaned/inconsistent data
- [ ] Background jobs are resumable after crash
- [ ] Cache invalidation works correctly on data mutation
- [ ] Database transactions commit/rollback correctly

### Integration
- [ ] All external API calls handle timeout and error responses
- [ ] Webhook receivers validate signatures and handle retries
- [ ] Message queue consumers handle duplicate messages (idempotent)
- [ ] File uploads handle max size, wrong type, corrupt files

### Performance
- [ ] No N+1 queries (verify with query logging)
- [ ] Response times within targets under expected load
- [ ] Memory usage stable over time (no leaks)
- [ ] Connection pools sized correctly and not exhausted
- [ ] Large dataset operations use streaming/pagination

---

## 16. POST-CODING: PENETRATION TESTING CHECKLIST

Test EVERY endpoint against these attack vectors before delivery:

### Injection Attacks
- [ ] SQL injection: `' OR 1=1--`, `'; DROP TABLE--`, union-based, time-based blind
- [ ] NoSQL injection: `{"$gt": ""}`, `{"$ne": null}`
- [ ] Command injection: `; ls -la`, `| cat /etc/passwd`, `` `whoami` ``
- [ ] XSS (stored, reflected, DOM): `<script>alert(1)</script>`, event handlers, SVG injection
- [ ] Template injection: `{{7*7}}`, `${7*7}`, `<%= 7*7 %>`
- [ ] LDAP injection (if applicable): `*)(uid=*))(|(uid=*`
- [ ] Path traversal: `../../../etc/passwd`, `..\..\..\windows\system.ini`
- [ ] Header injection: `\r\nX-Injected: true`

### Authentication & Session
- [ ] Brute force: verify lockout after 5-10 attempts
- [ ] Token manipulation: modify JWT payload, expired tokens, tokens signed with `none` algorithm
- [ ] Session fixation: verify session ID changes on login
- [ ] Credential stuffing: verify rate limiting blocks bulk attempts
- [ ] Password reset: verify token is single-use, expires, and doesn't leak in URL/referrer

### Authorization
- [ ] IDOR: access resources by changing IDs in URLs/body (`/api/users/123` -> `/api/users/124`)
- [ ] Privilege escalation: modify role field in request, access admin endpoints as user
- [ ] BOLA (Broken Object Level Auth): enumerate objects across tenant boundaries
- [ ] Function-level: access admin APIs without admin role
- [ ] Missing auth: find endpoints that don't check authentication at all

### SSRF (NEW in V3)
- [ ] Internal network access: submit `http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/` as URL parameter
- [ ] Cloud metadata: test AWS, GCP, Azure metadata endpoints
- [ ] DNS rebinding: use a domain that resolves to internal IP after first lookup
- [ ] Protocol smuggling: `gopher://`, `file://`, `dict://` schemes
- [ ] Redirect bypass: URL that redirects to internal host

### Business Logic
- [ ] Race conditions: simultaneous duplicate requests
- [ ] Integer overflow: very large quantities, negative prices
- [ ] Logic bypass: skip steps in multi-step flows
- [ ] Rate limit bypass: distribute across IPs, header manipulation

### Data Exposure
- [ ] Error messages: verify no stack traces, file paths, SQL queries, or internal IPs in responses
- [ ] Debug endpoints: verify `/debug/`, `/status/`, `/metrics/` are protected or removed
- [ ] Response headers: no `Server`, `X-Powered-By`, or version-revealing headers
- [ ] API responses: verify no extra fields beyond what's documented

---

## 17. WEBSOCKET SECURITY

### Connection Security
- [ ] `wss://` (TLS) enforced — no `ws://` in production
- [ ] Origin header validated against allowlist on upgrade request
- [ ] Authentication token validated on connection (via query param or first message) — NOT in URL path
- [ ] Per-IP connection limits enforced (default: 10 concurrent connections per IP)
- [ ] Connection timeout for idle connections (default: 5min without messages)

### Message Security
- [ ] Every incoming message validated against schema (type, size, format)
- [ ] Maximum message size enforced (default: 64KB — configurable per endpoint)
- [ ] Message rate limiting per connection (default: 60 messages/min)
- [ ] Binary messages rejected unless explicitly expected
- [ ] No dynamic code execution from message content

### State Management
- [ ] Connection state tracked server-side (authenticated user, subscriptions, permissions)
- [ ] Re-authentication on token refresh (don't trust stale tokens for long-lived connections)
- [ ] Subscription authorization: verify the user has permission to receive each event type
- [ ] Graceful disconnection on auth failure (close code 4001 with reason, no reconnect)

### WebSocket-Specific Attacks to Test
- [ ] CSWSH (Cross-Site WebSocket Hijacking): verify Origin validation
- [ ] Message injection: send malformed JSON, oversized messages, binary when text expected
- [ ] Connection flooding: verify per-IP limits prevent resource exhaustion
- [ ] Subscription escalation: subscribe to channels/events the user shouldn't access
- [ ] Replay attacks: resend captured legitimate messages

---

## 18. AI-SPECIFIC ANTI-PATTERNS TO AVOID

These are patterns that AI code generators produce frequently. **Reject any code matching these patterns.**

### 18.1: Tutorial-Style Code
```
BAD: Comments that explain basic syntax
// Create a new variable to store the user
const user = await db.findUser(id);
// Check if the user exists
if (!user) { ... }

GOOD: Comments that explain WHY, not WHAT
// Fail fast — downstream operations assume non-null user
const user = await db.findUser(id);
if (!user) { ... }
```

### 18.2: God Functions
AI often generates single 200+ line functions. **Always decompose:**
- Extract validation into a separate function
- Extract business logic into service methods
- Extract error handling into middleware
- Each function: one clear purpose, max 40 lines

### 18.3: Optimistic Data Access
```go
// BAD: AI assumes data exists
user := users[0] // What if slice is empty?
name := response.Data.User.Name // What if any level is nil?

// GOOD: Defensive access
if len(users) == 0 {
  return nil, ErrNoUsersFound
}
user := users[0]

if response.Data == nil || response.Data.User == nil {
  return nil, ErrMalformedResponse
}
name := response.Data.User.Name
```

### 18.4: Incomplete Error Handling
```go
// BAD: AI returns nil error or ignores error
result, _ := db.Query(ctx, query) // Error silently discarded
return result, nil

// GOOD: Every error handled
result, err := db.Query(ctx, query)
if err != nil {
  return nil, fmt.Errorf("querying users: %w", err)
}
return result, nil
```

### 18.5: Hardcoded Configuration
```go
// BAD: AI hardcodes values
client := redis.NewClient(&redis.Options{Addr: "localhost:6379"})
smtp.Send("smtp.gmail.com", 587, email)

// GOOD: Configuration via environment
client := redis.NewClient(&redis.Options{Addr: cfg.RedisAddr})
smtp.Send(cfg.SMTPHost, cfg.SMTPPort, email)
```

### 18.6: Phantom Imports
AI hallucinates packages that don't exist or are community forks of different packages.
```
BAD: import "github.com/someuser/jwt-go"        // Archived, use golang-jwt/jwt/v5
BAD: import "go.utils.validation"                 // Doesn't exist
BAD: import { validateEmail } from 'email-tools'  // Hallucinated package

GOOD: Verify every import resolves on the official registry before using
```

### 18.7: SQL String Interpolation
AI frequently generates string-interpolated SQL, especially in dynamic query builders.
```go
// BAD (AI favorite)
query := fmt.Sprintf("SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '%s'", name)

// GOOD (always parameterized)
query := "SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = $1"
rows, err := db.Query(ctx, query, name)
```

### 18.8: Catch-All Error Suppression
```typescript
// BAD: AI wraps everything in try/catch with generic handling
try {
  const result = await complexOperation();
  return result;
} catch (e) {
  console.log('error:', e); // Silent failure, no propagation
  return null;
}

// GOOD: Specific error handling with proper propagation
try {
  const result = await complexOperation();
  return result;
} catch (e) {
  if (e instanceof ValidationError) {
    throw new BadRequestError(e.message);
  }
  logger.error('complex operation failed', { error: e, correlationId: ctx.correlationId });
  throw new InternalError('operation failed', { cause: e });
}
```

### 18.9: Missing Cleanup / Resource Leaks
```go
// BAD: AI opens resources without defer/finally
file, err := os.Open(path)
// ... 50 lines of code ...
file.Close() // Never reached if error above

// GOOD: Immediate defer after successful open
file, err := os.Open(path)
if err != nil {
  return fmt.Errorf("opening file %s: %w", path, err)
}
defer file.Close()
```

### 18.10: Verbose Over-Engineering
AI adds unnecessary abstraction layers, factory patterns, and interface hierarchies for simple operations.
```
BAD: UserServiceFactory -> UserServiceImpl -> UserRepository -> UserRepositoryImpl
     (for a CRUD app with 3 endpoints)

GOOD: UserService -> UserRepository
      (add abstraction layers when you have >1 implementation or need testability)
```

### 18.11: Copy-Paste Duplication
AI repeats similar code blocks instead of extracting shared logic.
```
BAD: 5 handler functions each with identical auth + validation + error handling
GOOD: Middleware handles auth, validation helper extracts common patterns, handlers focus on business logic
```

### 18.12: Outdated Dependencies and Practices
AI suggests packages from its training data which may be archived, vulnerable, or superseded.
```
BAD:
- github.com/dgrijalva/jwt-go    (archived since 2021 — use golang-jwt/jwt/v5)
- github.com/pkg/errors           (unnecessary since Go 1.13 — use fmt.Errorf with %w)
- bcrypt for new projects          (use Argon2id)
- express-validator@4              (use v7+)
- request (npm)                    (deprecated — use got, node-fetch, or built-in fetch)

GOOD: Check pkg.go.dev / npmjs.com / packagist.org for current status before importing
```

### 18.13: Slopsquatting (AI-Hallucinated Package Names) (NEW in V3)
AI models hallucinate plausible-sounding package names that don't exist. Attackers monitor AI models, identify commonly hallucinated names, and register them on npm/PyPI/Go with malware payloads.

**Real examples of slopsquatted packages:**
- `huggingface-cli` (real: `huggingface_hub`)
- `python-postgres` (real: `psycopg2` or `asyncpg`)
- `@react-native/logger` (doesn't exist — scoped naming confusion)

**Defense:**
1. NEVER `npm install` / `go get` / `pip install` an AI-suggested package without first verifying it on the official registry
2. Check the package's GitHub/source repo — is it the official project?
3. Check download counts — legitimate packages have thousands of weekly downloads
4. Check creation date — packages created after your AI model's training cutoff are suspicious
5. When in doubt, use stdlib or write the functionality yourself
6. CI/CD: integrate `socket.dev` or `npm audit signatures` to detect tampered packages

---

## 19. DELIVERY CHECKLIST (FINAL GATE)

Before ANY code is committed, every item must be checked:

### Quality Gates (All 19 must pass)
- [ ] QG-01: Readable — Clean architecture, SRP, max 3 nesting, max 40-line functions
- [ ] QG-02: Secure — OWASP 2025 compliant, SSRF prevented, WebSocket secured
- [ ] QG-03: Typed — Full type safety, no `any`/`interface{}`
- [ ] QG-04: Structured — Typed errors on every path, no empty catches
- [ ] QG-05: Validated — Server-side schema validation on all boundaries
- [ ] QG-06: DRY — No duplicated logic
- [ ] QG-07: Defensive — Null/empty/boundary/concurrent handling
- [ ] QG-08: Logged — Structured JSON, correlation IDs, trace context, zero PII
- [ ] QG-09: Bounded — Timeouts, circuit breakers, retry with backoff
- [ ] QG-10: Clean — Linter passes, no debug code, consistent formatting
- [ ] QG-11: Documented — Doc comments, README, INSTALL, USAGE, ARCHITECTURE, RUNBOOK
- [ ] QG-12: Configurable — Env vars or secret manager, no hardcoded values
- [ ] QG-13: Compatible — Backwards compatible or explicit versioning with migration guide
- [ ] QG-14: Tested — 80%+ coverage, meaningful assertions, race detection
- [ ] QG-15: Dependency-Safe — Verified, maintained, pinned, no hallucinated packages
- [ ] QG-16: Performant — No N+1, pagination, no memory leaks, algorithmic efficiency
- [ ] QG-17: Provenance-Verified — SLSA L2+, SBOM generated, artifacts signed
- [ ] QG-18: Observable — Distributed tracing, metrics, health probes, alerting rules
- [ ] QG-19: Deployment-Safe — Hardened containers, graceful shutdown, zero-downtime migrations

### Final Checks
- [ ] No `TODO`, `FIXME`, or placeholder code
- [ ] No commented-out code
- [ ] No `console.log`, `fmt.Println`, or debug statements
- [ ] All environment variables documented in `.env.example`
- [ ] Lock files committed (`go.sum`, `package-lock.json`, `composer.lock`)
- [ ] CI pipeline passes (lint + test + security scan + SBOM generation)
- [ ] Breaking changes documented with migration guide
- [ ] README updated with any new setup steps or dependencies
- [ ] RUNBOOK updated with any new failure modes or recovery procedures

## 20. LANGUAGE-SPECIFIC APPENDICES

### Appendix A: Go (1.22+)

**Project Structure:**
```
cmd/
  server/main.go        # Entry point — minimal, just wiring
internal/
  config/               # Configuration loading
  handler/              # HTTP handlers (thin — call services)
  service/              # Business logic
  repository/           # Data access layer
  middleware/           # HTTP middleware
  model/                # Domain models and DTOs
  errors/               # Custom error types
pkg/                    # Public, reusable packages (if any)
migrations/             # SQL migrations (numbered: 001_create_users.up.sql)
api/                    # OpenAPI specs, protobuf definitions
```

**Go-Specific Rules:**
- Use `internal/` to prevent external package imports
- `context.Context` as first parameter in all functions that do I/O
- Error wrapping with `fmt.Errorf("doing X: %w", err)` — preserves error chain
- Use `errors.Is()` and `errors.As()` for error checking — never string comparison
- `slog` (Go 1.21+) for structured logging: `slog.Error("query failed", "err", err, "query", queryName)`
- `go vet`, `staticcheck`, `golangci-lint` in CI — zero warnings policy
- `go test -race -count=1 ./...` in CI — race detector on, no test caching
- Use `goleak` to detect goroutine leaks in tests
- `go mod tidy` must not change `go.sum` on CI (ensures reproducible builds)

**Go 1.22+ Features to Use:**
- Range-over-int: `for i := range 10 { ... }` (replaces `for i := 0; i < 10; i++`)
- Loop variable fix: loop variables are per-iteration (no more capture bugs)
- Enhanced routing: `http.NewServeMux()` with method patterns: `mux.HandleFunc("GET /api/users/{id}", handler)`
- `slices` and `maps` packages for generic collection operations

**Go Security Checklist:**
- [ ] `gosec` passes with zero findings
- [ ] `govulncheck` reports no known vulnerabilities
- [ ] No `unsafe` package usage without explicit security review
- [ ] All `defer` cleanup runs even on panic (test with `recover`)
- [ ] Context cancellation propagated to all goroutines
- [ ] `crypto/rand` used for all randomness (never `math/rand`)

### Appendix B: TypeScript (5.5+)

**Project Structure:**
```
src/
  config/               # Configuration and validation
  controllers/          # Route handlers (thin)
  services/             # Business logic
  repositories/         # Data access
  middleware/           # Express/Fastify middleware
  models/               # Types, interfaces, schemas
  utils/                # Shared utilities
  errors/               # Custom error classes
  types/                # Shared type definitions
```

**TypeScript-Specific Rules:**
- `strict: true` in `tsconfig.json` — no exceptions
- `noUncheckedIndexedAccess: true` — array/object access returns `T | undefined`
- **Zero `any` tolerance** — use `unknown` with type narrowing, generics, or explicit types
- Use `satisfies` operator for type-safe object literals: `const config = { ... } satisfies Config`
- Use discriminated unions for state machines:
  ```typescript
  type ConnectionState =
    | { status: 'connecting' }
    | { status: 'connected'; sessionId: string }
    | { status: 'error'; error: Error; retryCount: number };
  ```
- Zod for runtime schema validation on all external boundaries
- ESLint with `@typescript-eslint/strict-type-checked` ruleset — zero warnings
- Prefer `node:` prefix for Node.js built-ins: `import { readFile } from 'node:fs/promises'`

**TypeScript Security Checklist:**
- [ ] `npm audit` reports zero critical/high vulnerabilities
- [ ] No `eval()`, `Function()`, or `vm.runInNewContext()` with user input
- [ ] All `process.env` access via validated config module (never raw access in business logic)
- [ ] `helmet` middleware configured for Express/Fastify
- [ ] CSP headers set with nonces for inline scripts
- [ ] No prototype pollution vectors (use `Object.create(null)` for dictionaries or `Map`)

### Appendix C: PHP (8.3+)

**Project Structure (Laravel/Symfony):**
```
app/
  Http/Controllers/     # Thin controllers
  Services/             # Business logic
  Repositories/         # Data access
  Models/               # Eloquent models with strict typing
  Middleware/            # Request middleware
  Exceptions/           # Custom exception classes
  Rules/                # Custom validation rules
config/                 # Configuration files
database/migrations/    # Timestamped migrations
tests/
  Unit/
  Feature/
```

**PHP-Specific Rules:**
- `declare(strict_types=1)` at the top of EVERY file
- Type declarations for all function parameters, return types, and properties
- PHPStan level 8 minimum — zero errors
- Use enums (PHP 8.1+) instead of string/int constants
- Use `readonly` properties and classes where applicable
- Named arguments for functions with >3 parameters
- Fiber for async operations where applicable (PHP 8.1+)
- Use match expressions instead of switch for value returns

**PHP Security Checklist:**
- [ ] `composer audit` reports zero known vulnerabilities
- [ ] Prepared statements for ALL database queries (no string interpolation)
- [ ] CSRF protection enabled on all state-changing routes
- [ ] File uploads validated: check MIME type from content (not extension), rename on storage, store outside web root
- [ ] `htmlspecialchars()` with `ENT_QUOTES | ENT_SUBSTITUTE` for all user-generated output
- [ ] `password_hash()` with `PASSWORD_ARGON2ID` for new password storage
- [ ] Session configuration: `strict` mode, `httponly`, `secure`, regenerate ID on privilege change

## 21. CONTAINER & DEPLOYMENT SECURITY (NEW in V3)

> **Entirely new section.** V2 had zero guidance on deployment security. Code can pass all quality gates and still ship in a root container with secrets in the image. This section closes that gap.

### Container Image Hardening

**Mandatory rules for all container images:**

1. **Minimal base images:**
   ```dockerfile
   # GOOD: Distroless or Alpine
   FROM gcr.io/distroless/static-debian12:nonroot
   FROM golang:1.23-alpine AS builder

   # BAD: Full OS images
   # FROM ubuntu:22.04
   # FROM node:20
   ```

2. **Multi-stage builds** to minimize attack surface:
   ```dockerfile
   # Build stage
   FROM golang:1.23-alpine AS builder
   WORKDIR /build
   COPY go.mod go.sum ./
   RUN go mod download
   COPY . .
   RUN CGO_ENABLED=0 GOOS=linux go build -ldflags='-s -w' -o /app ./cmd/server

   # Runtime stage — no compiler, no source code, no build tools
   FROM gcr.io/distroless/static-debian12:nonroot
   COPY --from=builder /app /app
   USER nonroot:nonroot
   EXPOSE 8080
   ENTRYPOINT ["/app"]
   ```

3. **Non-root execution:**
   - ALL containers run as non-root user (UID >= 1000)
   - No `--privileged` flag
   - No `CAP_SYS_ADMIN` or unnecessary Linux capabilities
   - File permissions: application binary owned by root, executed by non-root

4. **No secrets in images:**
   - NEVER `COPY .env`, `COPY credentials.json`, or `ARG PASSWORD=...` in Dockerfile
   - Use runtime secret injection (K8s Secrets, Vault Agent, env vars from orchestrator)
   - Multi-stage builds: secrets used only in build stage, never copied to runtime stage
   - Scan images with `trivy` or `grype` for accidentally included secrets

5. **Image scanning in CI:**
   ```yaml
   # GitHub Actions — scan on every build
   - uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@master
     with:
       image-ref: 'ghcr.io/myorg/myapp:${{ github.sha }}'
       severity: 'CRITICAL,HIGH'
       exit-code: '1'  # Fail build on findings
   ```

### Kubernetes Security

**Pod Security Standards (Restricted):**
```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: myapp
spec:
  securityContext:
    runAsNonRoot: true
    runAsUser: 1000
    runAsGroup: 1000
    fsGroup: 1000
    seccompProfile:
      type: RuntimeDefault
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: ghcr.io/myorg/myapp:v1.2.3@sha256:abc123...  # Pin by digest
    securityContext:
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
      readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
      capabilities:
        drop: ["ALL"]
    resources:
      requests:
        cpu: "100m"
        memory: "128Mi"
      limits:
        cpu: "500m"
        memory: "512Mi"
    livenessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /healthz
        port: 8080
      initialDelaySeconds: 5
      periodSeconds: 10
    readinessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /readyz
        port: 8080
      initialDelaySeconds: 5
      periodSeconds: 5
```

**Kubernetes hardening checklist:**
- [ ] All pods run as non-root with `runAsNonRoot: true`
- [ ] Read-only root filesystem with `readOnlyRootFilesystem: true`
- [ ] All capabilities dropped with `drop: ["ALL"]`
- [ ] No privilege escalation with `allowPrivilegeEscalation: false`
- [ ] Resource requests AND limits defined for CPU and memory
- [ ] Images pinned by SHA digest (not mutable tags like `:latest`)
- [ ] Network policies restrict pod-to-pod communication (default deny, explicit allow)
- [ ] RBAC: service accounts have minimal permissions (no cluster-admin)
- [ ] Secrets encrypted at rest (etcd encryption or external KMS)
- [ ] Liveness and readiness probes configured on every container
- [ ] `terminationGracePeriodSeconds` matches application's shutdown timeout
- [ ] Seccomp profile set to `RuntimeDefault` minimum

### Deployment Strategy

**Zero-downtime deployments:**
- Rolling update strategy with `maxSurge: 1` and `maxUnavailable: 0`
- Readiness probe must pass before receiving traffic
- Graceful shutdown must complete before pod termination
- Database migrations run as separate job BEFORE deployment (not as init container)
- Feature flags for safe rollback without redeployment

**Deployment checklist:**
- [ ] Container image built, scanned, and signed
- [ ] SBOM attached to the release
- [ ] Database migrations tested against staging with production-size data
- [ ] Rollback procedure documented and tested
- [ ] Monitoring dashboards updated for new metrics/endpoints
- [ ] Alerting rules deployed alongside the service
- [ ] Runbook updated with new failure modes

---

## APPENDIX: QUICK REFERENCE CARD

### Timeouts
| Resource | Default Timeout |
|----------|-----------------|
| HTTP client call | 10s |
| Database query (OLTP) | 5s |
| Database query (reporting) | 30s |
| gRPC unary call | 5s |
| gRPC streaming | 60s |
| Background job step | 5min |
| Graceful shutdown | 30s |
| WebSocket idle | 5min |
| Circuit breaker cooldown | 30s |
| Health check interval | 10s |

### Password Hashing
| Algorithm | Status | Notes |
|-----------|--------|-------|
| Argon2id | **Required (new code)** | No input length limits |
| bcrypt | Legacy only | 72-byte truncation — migration required |
| scrypt | Acceptable | Alternative to Argon2id |
| MD5/SHA-1/SHA-256 (plain) | **BANNED** | Never for passwords |

### HTTP Status Codes (API)
| Code | Meaning | When |
|------|---------|------|
| 200 | OK | Successful read or update |
| 201 | Created | Successful resource creation |
| 204 | No Content | Successful delete |
| 400 | Bad Request | Validation error |
| 401 | Unauthorized | Missing or invalid auth |
| 403 | Forbidden | Valid auth, insufficient permissions |
| 404 | Not Found | Resource doesn't exist |
| 409 | Conflict | State conflict (duplicate, version mismatch) |
| 422 | Unprocessable | Syntactically valid but semantically wrong |
| 429 | Too Many Requests | Rate limited |
| 500 | Internal Error | Unexpected server failure |
| 503 | Service Unavailable | Temporarily down, retry later |

### Circuit Breaker Defaults
| Parameter | Default | Override |
|-----------|---------|----------|
| Failure threshold | 5 consecutive | Project-specific in kickstart |
| Cooldown (half-open) | 30s | Service-dependent |
| Success threshold (close) | 2 consecutive | — |
| Timeout per call | Matches resource timeout | — |
